title: allex2901
body:
UNSW School of Mathematics and Statistics MATH2901 Higher Theory of Statistics T UTORIAL EXERCISES

Contents 1 Randomness, Variability, Uncertainty, Probability 2

2 Elements of Probability theory 4

3 Random variables 8

4 Random vectors 13

5 Common distributions 19

6 Transformations of random variables 24

7 Introduction to statistical inference 26

8 Estimation methods 28

9 Confidence intervals 31

10 Hypothesis testing 35  1 Randomness, Variability, Uncertainty, Probability 1.1. (a) You flip a fair coin. Before it lands, what is the probability that it will show up Heads? Justify your answer.

(b) After the coin lands on the table, you immediately cover it with your hand so that no one (including yourself) can see the result. Would it still be meaningful to say that ‘there is a probability 1/2 of observing Heads’? Why or why not?

(Hint: The answer to this question is more philosophical than technical, and does not directly depend on the value 1/2. Rather, it invites reflection on different interpretations of probability and is intended to prompt you to consider what the concept of ‘probability’ truly means.)

1.2. Suppose that, in a given population, 1 in 100 people has a certain disease. A test for this disease is 90% accurate, meaning that:

• 90% of those who have the disease will test positive; • 10% of those who do not have the disease will also test positive (a false positive result).

(a) Consider the case where a person is selected totally at random from the population, tested for the disease, and receives a positive result. They might be tempted to conclude: ‘I tested positive using a test that is 90% accurate, so there is a 90% chance that I have the disease’.

Carefully analyse why this reasoning may be flawed. (We assume that the only information available about this person is that they were chosen totally at random and that their test result was positive; in particular, they are not someone who specifically sought testing due to symptoms or concerns.)

(b) Now, imagine that this scenario has actually occurred: a specific individual – let’s say, Homer Simpson – has been selected and has tested positive. Can we meaningfully state that Homer has a probability α (for some α ∈ [0, 1] that may be determined) of having the disease?

1.3. A book has exactly 1,000 pages, numbered from 1 to 1,000. You pick up a copy of the book and, without looking, tear out one page totally at random. You then close the book and give it to a friend, who does not know which page was removed.

(a) Before checking the book, what is the probability that page 42 is missing? Justify your answer.

(b) Your friend flips through the book and confirms that page 500 is still there. Does this change the probability that page 42 is missing? Why or why not?

(c) Imagine that your friend now carefully checks pages 501 through 1,000 and finds that none are missing. What probability should they now assign to page 42 being the missing one?

(d) Discuss how this problem relates to different ways of interpreting probability.

1.4. (a) A fair coin is flipped five times in a row, and each time it lands on Heads. A gambler believes that the next flip is more likely to be Tails to ‘balance out’ the results. Explain why this reasoning is incorrect. (b) The above misconception is often referred to as the gambler’s fallacy. It is often observed in real-world scenarios, such as gambling, financial markets, or sports predictions. Can you think of an example where people might fall into this trap? (c) If instead of five flips, the coin landed on Heads 100 times in a row, would you still argue that the next flip is equally likely to be Heads or Tails? Does your intuition change, and if so, why?

1.5. (a) Consider measuring the height of a group of people. What are some sources of variability in those measurements? If you were to repeatedly select groups of n = 10 people totally at random from a population (what will later be called ‘random sampling’), would you expect to see the same values for their height in each of those samples? Why or why not? (b) A weather forecast states that there is a 70% chance of rain tomorrow. What does this mean? What does it not mean? If it does not rain tomorrow, does that mean the forecast was wrong? Why or why not? (c) A survey finds that 60% of a randomly selected sample of n persons (n ∈ N) in a given population supports a policy. If another random sample of size is taken, do you expect to get the same percentage? Why or why not? How does increasing the sample size affect the variability of the observed proportion?

2 Elements of Probability theory 2.1. Let S be the sample space of a random experiment, and A, B, C be three events. Using unions, intersections and complements, find expressions defining the events: (a) only A occurs, (b) at least one event occurs, (c) at least two events occur, (d) exactly one event occurs, (e) exactly two events occur. 2.2. Prove Boole’s inequalities: ! n [ n X P Ei ≤ P(Ei ) i=1 i=1

and ! n \ n X P Ei ≥1− P(Eic ). i=1 i=1

(Hint: For the first inequality, proceed by induction. Show that this is true for n = 1 and then that this is true for n + 1 if true for n. For the second inequality, use the first one and the fact that the complement of a union is the intersection of the complements.) 2.3. (a) If an event E1 is independent of itself, show that P(E1 ) is either 0 or 1. (b) If P(E1 ) is 0 or 1, show that E1 is independent of all other events. (Hint: Start from the basic definition of independence of events.) 2.4. Let E1 and E2 be independent events. Show that E1c and E2 are independent, and deduce that E1c and E2c are also independent. (Hint: Start from the basic definition of independence of events.) 2.5. Let E1 and E2 be two events such that P(E1 )×P(E2 ) ̸= 0. Show that, if P(E1 |E2 ) > P(E1 ), then P(E2 |E1 ) > P(E2 ). (Hint: Use Bayes’ first rule.) 2.6. Employment data at a large company reveal that 72% of the workers are married, that 44% are university graduates, and that half of the university graduates are married. What is the probability that a randomly chosen worker (a) is neither married nor a university graduate? (b) is married but not a university graduate? (c) is married or a university graduate? (Answer: (a) 0.06, (b) 0.5, (c) 0.94) 2.7. The following is a table of the annual promotion probabilities at a particular workplace, broken down by gender.

Promoted Not promoted Total Male 0.17 0.68 0.85 Female 0.03 0.12 0.15

Is there gender bias in promotion? (i.e., is getting promoted independent of gender?) (Hint: compare conditional and unconditional probabilities of getting promoted.)

2.8. A market researcher observes that four customers purchased Product A from a local store, six customers purchased Product B from a local store, and six customers purchased Product A from an online store. How many customers must have purchased Product B from the online store for product type and store type to be independent when a customer is selected at random? (Answer: 9)

2.9. The probability of winning on a single toss of the dice is p (0 < p < 1). A starts, and if she fails, she passes the dice to B, who then attempts to win on her toss. They continue tossing the dice back and forth until one of them wins. What are their respective probabilities of winning? (Answer: P(A wins) = 1/(2 − p), P(B wins) = (1 − p)/(2 − p))

2.10. A plays tennis against B. During a given game, the score reaches ‘deuce’. Each player then needs to score two more points than the other to win the game. Assuming that each point is independently won by A with probability p, what is the probability they will have to play a total of 2n points to end the game? What is the probability that A will win the game? p2 (Answer: P(2n points are needed) = (2p(1 − p))n−1 (p2 + (1 − p)2 ), P(A wins) = 1−2p(1−p) )

2.11. Suppose that two balanced dice are tossed repeatedly and the sum of the two uppermost faces is determined on each toss. What is the probability that we obtain a sum of 3 before we obtain a sum of 7? (Answer: 1/4)

2.12. Suppose we have ten coins which are such that if the ith coin is flipped then heads will appear with probability i/10 (i = 1, 2, . . . , 10). When one of the coins is randomly selected and flipped, it shows heads. What is the conditional probability that it was the fifth coin? (Answer: 1/11)

2.13. Urn 1 contains 2 red balls and 3 black balls. Urn 2 contains 4 red balls and 5 black balls.

(a) If an urn is randomly selected and a ball drawn at random from it, what is the probability that the ball is red? (b) Suppose a ball is drawn at random from urn 1 and placed into urn 2. If a ball is then drawn at random from the 10 balls in urn 2, what is the probability that it is red? (c) In the previous question, given that the ball drawn from urn 2 is red, what is the probability that the ball transferred from urn 1 was black?

(Answer: (a) 19/45, (b) 11/25, (c) 6/11)

2.14. Down’s Syndrome is a disorder that affects 1 in 270 babies that have mothers aged 35 or over. According to a study in ‘The New England Journal of Medicine’, the sensitivity of the

new blood test for Down’s Syndrome (i.e., the probability of a positive test result, given that Down’s Syndrome is present) is 89%; while its specificity (i.e., the probability of a negative result, given that Down’s Syndrome is absent) is 75%.

(a) What is the probability that a woman over age 35 would return positive on the new blood test? (b) A mother over age 35 returns a positive test result. What is the probability that Down’s Syndrome is actually present? (c) A mother over age 35 returns a negative test result. What is the probability that Down’s Syndrome is actually present?

(Answer: (a) 0.2524, (b) 0.013, (c) 0.00054)

2.15. Police often set up drink-drive checkpoints — roadblocks where drivers are asked a few brief questions to allow the officer to judge whether or not the person may have been drinking. If the officer does not suspect a problem, drivers are released to continue. Otherwise, drivers are detained for a Breathalyser test that will determine whether or not they are arrested for being over the legal blood-alcohol limit. The police say that based on the brief initial stop, trained officers can make the right decision 80% of the time (i.e., 80% chance of testing someone who has been drinking, and 80% chance of non testing someone who hasn’t been drinking). Suppose the police operate a drink-drive checkpoint after 9 o’clock on a Saturday night, a time when national traffic experts suspect that about 12% of drivers are over the legal blood-alcohol limit.

(a) You are stopped at the checkpoint and have not been drinking. What is the probability that you are given a Breathalyser test? (b) What is the probability that any given driver will be tested? (c) What is the probability that a driver who is tested is actually over the legal bloodalcohol limit? (d) What is the probability that a driver who is released without being tested had actually been over the legal blood-alcohol limit?

(Answer: (a) 0.2, (b) 0.272, (c) 0.353, (d) 0.033)

2.16. You are imprisoned in a dungeon together with two fellow prisoners. You are informed by the jailer that one of you has been chosen at random to be hanged, and the other two are to be freed. You ask the jailer to tell you privately which of your fellow prisoners will be set free, claiming that there would be no harm in divulging this information, since you already know that at least one of them will go free.

(a) The jailer refuses to answer the question, pointing out that if you knew which of your fellows were to be set free, then your own probability of being hanged would rise from 1/3 to 1/2, since you would then be one of two prisoners. Show that, under reasonable assumptions, the jailer is wrong. (Hint: Show that P(you hanged) = P(you hanged | whatever the jailer says))

(b) You convince the jailer, and he tells you which of the other two will be set free. You say this information to your fellow prisoners, and while the spared guy is jumping for joy, the other one asks you to switch your identities. Would you accept? (Hint: What is his probability of being hanged?)

2.17. A system has n independent components and each fails with probability p. Calculate the probability that the system will fail when

(a) the components are in series, so the system fails if any of the components fail, (b) the components are in parallel, so the system fails only when all of the components fail.

2.18. Suppose a room contains n people. Assume that the 365 days of the year are equally likely to be birthdays for each person.

(a) Calculate the probability that at least two of the people have a common birthday. (b) What is the probability when n = 23? (Answer: 0.507) (c) How many people must you ask in order to have at least 50% chance of finding someone who shares your birthday? (Answer: 253)

2.19. You have to select at random one alternative between two with a probability exactly equal to 1/2. Unfortunately, all you have at your disposal is a coin that you suspect to be biased. Denote p the (unknown) probability of getting heads when it is flipped. Consider the following procedure:

1) Flip the coin, and let R1 , either Heads or Tails, be the result. 2) Flip the coin again, and let R2 be the result. 3) If R1 and R2 are the same, return to step 1; otherwise proceed to step 4. 4) If R2 is heads, choose alternative 1, otherwise choose alternative 2.

Show that, using this strategy, either alternative will be selected with exact probability 1/2, regardless of the value of p ∈ (0, 1).

2.20. Show that it is not possible to weight two dice in such a way that the sum of the two numbers shown by these loaded dice is equally likely to take any value between 2 and 12 (inclusive). (Hint: Write fi = P(X = i) and gi = P(Y = i), i = 1, . . . , 6, where X is the number shown on the first die and Y the number shown on the second die. Then, write the events P(X + Y = 2), P(X + Y = 7) and P(X + Y = 12) in terms of the fi ’s and gi ’s and show that P(X + Y = 2) = P(X + Y = 12) = P(X + Y = 7) = 1/11 leads to a contradiction.)

3 Random variables 3.1. Consider the game “rock, paper, scissors" in which two players instantaneously choose one of rock, paper and scissors. If both players pick the same item, they play again. If players make different choices, one of the two players wins (rock beats scissors, scissors beats paper, paper beats rock). Let X be the number of times the game is played until someone wins. Find the probability mass function of X, when the players are choosing amongst rock, paper and scissors totally at random.

3.2. Let X be the time it takes Jane to get home from UNSW. She catches a bus to Central, then a train to Westmead. On average, it takes her about 1 hour. The quickest she can get home is in 40 minutes, and 25% of the time she gets home in less than 52 minutes. Every now and then, there are delays and it takes as long as a couple of hours to get home. Draw a plausible probability density function fX (x) for this random variable X, using the above information. Where possible, indicate the above information on your diagram. Also, consider issues such as symmetry/skewness, unimodality/multimodality, etc.

3.3. An experiment consists of throwing a fair coin four times. Find the frequency function (probability mass function) and the cumulative distribution function of the following random variables:

(a) The number of heads before the first tail. (b) The number of heads following the first tail. (c) The number of heads minus the number of tails. (d) The number of tails times the number of heads.

3.4. Let X be a discrete random variable whose behaviour is driven by the probability mass function: c pX (x) = , x = 0, 1, 2, . . . x! for some constant c.

(a) Determine c. (Answer: e−1 ) (b) What is P(X = 2)? (Answer: 1/(2e)) (c) What is P(X < 2)? (Answer: 2/e) (d) What is P(X ≥ 4)? (Answer: 1 − 8/(3e)) (e) Obtain an expression for FX (x), and sketch it for small x.

3.5. A university lecturer never finishes their lecture before the end of the hour but always finishes within 2 minutes after the hour. Let X be the time that elapses between the end of the hour and the end of the lecture and suppose that the probability density of X is  kx2 0 < x < 2 fX (x) = 0 otherwise

(a) What is the value of k? (Answer: k = 3/8) (b) What is the cumulative distribution function of X? (c) What is the probability that the lecture continues beyond the hour for between 60 and 90 seconds? (Answer: 0.2969)

3.6. Let X be a continuous random variable with cumulative distribution function F (x) and density f (x) = F ′ (x). Find the probability density function of

(a) the maximum, say X(n) , of n independent random variables, each of which with distribution function F . (Answer: fX(n) (x) = n (F (x))n−1 f (x)) (b) the minimum, say X(1) , of n independent random variables, each of which with distribution function F . (Answer: fX(1) (x) = n (1 − F (x))n−1 f (x))

3.7. Let X be a continuous random variable with a probability density function fX (x) that is symmetric about some point ξ, i.e.,

fX (ξ − x) = fX (ξ + x) for all x ∈ R.

Show that if E(X) exists, then E(X) = ξ.

3.8. Suppose that a point is chosen at random in the interior of a circle of radius 1. Let D be the distance of the selected point from the centre of the circle.

(a) What is the density function of D? (Answer: fD (x) = 2x, for x ∈ [0, 1]) (b) Find E(D) and Var(D). (Answer: E(D) = 2/3, Var(D) = 1/18)

3.9. Trees in a certain woodlot have basal diameter X (in metres) whose distribution admits the following pdf: fX (x) = 1I{0≤x≤1} . (Recall that 1I{•} is the indicator function, taking the value 1 if • is true, and 0 otherwise.)

(a) Find the average basal diameter of the tree trunks. (Answer: 1/2)

(b) Find the average basal area of the tree trunks. (Answer: π/12) (c) The trees have height 5 metres, and their timber currently fetches $30 per cubic metre when sold. Assuming cylindrical trunks, calculate the average value of trees in the woodlot. (Answer: 25π/2) (d) Over the next year, it is expected that all trees will increase their basal area by 0.1 square metres, and their heights will increase to 6 metres. Find the average value of trees in the woodlot in a year’s time (according to projections). (Answer: 15π + 18)

3.10. The probability density function of the weight X (in kg) of packages delivered by a post office is fX (x) = for 1 < x < 70 69x2 and 0 elsewhere.

(a) In the long-term, what is the proportion of packages whose weight exceeds 50 kg? (b) Determine the mean and the variance of the weight X. (c) If the shipping cost is $2.50 per kg, what is the average shipping cost of a package? What is the variance of the shipping cost?

(Answer: (a) 0.0058, (b) E(X) = 4.31 kg, Var(X) = 51.42 kg2 , (c) E(cost) = 10.78$, Var(cost) = 321.4$2 )

3.11. Let X have the cumulative distribution function

FX (x) = 1 − x−α , x ≥ 1,

for some α > 0.

(a) Find E(X) for those values of α for which it exists. (b) Find Var(X) for those values of α for which it exists.

3.12. Let X be a continuous random variable with probability density function fX (x) = 1I{1≤x≤2} . Show that   1 1 E ̸= . X E(X)

3.13. Let f1 (x) and f2 (x) be probability density functions, and let a be a constant such that 0 ≤ a ≤ 1. Consider the function f (x) = af1 (x) + (1 − a)f2 (x).

a) Show that f (x) is a density function. Such a density is often referred to as a mixture of two densities. R (Hint: Show that f (x) ≥ 0 for all x and f (x) dx = 1)

b) Suppose that Xi (i = 1, 2) is a random variable with density fi (x), and that E(Xi ) = µi and Var(Xi ) = σi2 . Assume that X is random variable whose density is a mixture of the densities corresponding to X1 and X2 . i) Show that E(X) = aµ1 + (1 − a)µ2 ; ii) Show that Var(X) = aσ12 + (1 − a)σ22 + a(1 − a)(µ1 − µ2 )2 . R R (Hint: write explicitly xf (x) dx and x2 f (x) dx)

3.14. In 1994 Australia eliminated 1 and 2 cent coins, and thereafter it was intended that all transactions be rounded to the nearest 5 cents. Let X be the additional amount (in cents) that a shopkeeper makes from rounding transactions to the nearest 5 cents. Assuming that the number of cents for transactions is uniformly distributed, X has probability mass function:  1 x ∈ SX = {−2, −1, 0, 1, 2} pX (x) = 0 otherwise (a) A shopkeeper wants to know if he has been losing money, because of the change to the 5 cents system. What would you tell him? Include E(X) in your answer. (b) Amounts to be paid are rounded to the nearest 5 cents when paid for in cash. If paid for electronically (via credit card or EFTPOS), the exact amount is charged. You can abuse the rounding system by paying for transactions in cash when the amount will be rounded down, and paying electronically when the amount would have been rounded up. Let Y be the amount of money you save by using this approach, in a transaction that would be rounded to the nearest 5 cents if paid for in cash. i. Identify the distribution Y . ii. What is the expected amount of money you expect to gain per transaction by adopting this approach? (Answer: E(Y ) = 0.6 cents)

3.15. You roll a conventional die repeatedly. If it shows 1, you must stop, but you may choose to stop at any prior time. Your score is the number shown by the die on the final roll. Consider the strategy: stop the first time that the die shows r or greater. a) What stopping strategy (i.e. what value of r) yields the greatest expected score? b) What strategy would you use if your score was the square of the final roll? (Answer: a) stop at the first throw showing 4 or more; b) stop at the first throw showing 5 or more.)

3.16. A merchant stocks a certain perishable item. She knows that on any given day she will have a demand for either two, three, or four of these items with probabilities 0.1, 0.4 and 0.5, respectively. She buys the items for $10 each and sells them for $12 each. If any are left at the end of the day, they are lost. How many items should the merchant stock in order to maximize her expected daily profit? (Answer: 3 items should be stocked, E(profit) = 4.80 $)

3.17. Suppose that X takes on each of the values 1, 2, 3 with probability 1/3. What is the moment generating function? Find E(X), E(X 2 ) and E(X 3 ) by differentiating, and then compare with a direct derivation of these moments. (Answer: φX (s) = 31 (es + e2s + e3s ))  3.18. Let KX (s) = log φX (s) = log(E esX ), and show that ′ ′′ KX (0) = E(X), KX (0) = Var(X).

The function KX is called the cumulant generating function of the random variable X, and sometimes used instead of φX to represent interesting features of the distribution of X. (Hint: Differentiate KX (s) and use the properties of φX (s))

3.19. A type of bacteria cell divides at a constant rate λ over time (that is, the probability that a cell divides in a small interval of time t is approximately λt). Given that a population starts out at time zero with k cells of this bacteria and that cell divisions are independent of one another, the size of the population at time t, say X(t), has the probability distribution   n − 1 −λkt P(X(t) = n) = e (1 − e−λt )n−k , n = k, k + 1, . . . k−1

a) Find the expected value of X(t) in terms of λ, k and t. Hint: +∞ i+k 1 P  i i=0 i x = (1−x) k+1

for |x| < 1. (Answer: E(X(t)) = keλt ) b) If, for a type of bacteria cell, λ = 0.1 per second and the population starts out with two cells at time zero, find the expected value of the population after one minute. (Answer: 806.9 bacterias)

4 Random vectors 4.1. Let (X1 , X2 ) be a discrete random vector where X1 and X2 each take on either the value 1 or −1, with E(X1 ) = E(X2 ) = 0. Show that

a) pX1 X2 (1, 1) = pX1 X2 (−1, −1); (Hint: what is E(X1 + X2 )?) b) pX1 X2 (1, −1) = pX1 X2 (−1, 1) (Hint: what is E(X1 − X2 )?)

Let p = 2pX1 X2 (1, 1). Find

c) Var(X1 ); (Answer: 1 ) d) Var(X2 ); (Answer: 1) e) Cov(X1 , X2 ) (Answer: 2p − 1 )

4.2. Ascertain in the following cases whether or not F is the joint distribution function of some pair (X, Y ) of random variables. If your conclusion is affirmative, find the marginal cumulative distribution functions of X and Y .

a) 1 − e−x1 −x2 if x1 , x2 ≥ 0  F (x1 , x2 ) = 0 otherwise b)   1 − e−x1 − x1 e−x2 if 0 ≤ x1 ≤ x2 F (x1 , x2 ) = 1 − e−x2 − x2 e−x2 if 0 ≤ x2 ≤ x1 0 otherwise 

(Hint: Show that the second-order mixed partial derivative ∂x∂1 ∂x F satisfies the conditions to be a joint probability density function.)

4.3. A bivariate discrete random vector (X1 , X2 ) has joint probability mass function as follows:

X2 -1 0 1 0 0.01 0.08 0.01 X1 1 0.05 0.42 0.03 2 0.04 0.30 0.06

(a) Determine pX1 and pX2 . (b) Calculate E(X1 ), P(X1 = 1|X2 = 1), E(X1 |X2 = 1) and P(X1 + X2 = 1). (Answer: 1.3; 0.3; 1.5; 0.47) (c) Are X1 and X2 independent?

4.4. In a supermarket, there is an express lane and there is a normal queue. Let X1 be the queue length of an express lane, X2 is the length of a normal queue. On a day on which business is slow, queue lengths follow the following distribution:

X 2 = 0 X2 = 1 X 2 = 2 X1 = 0 0.07 0.06 0.05 X1 = 1 0.08 0.15 0.08 X1 = 2 0.10 0.11 0.30

(a) What is the chance that there are people in the express lane, but the normal lane is empty? (Answer: 0.18 ) (b) Find the expected total number of people queueing. (Answer: 2.51 ) (c) Find the marginal distribution of normal queue length. (d) If it is known that the normal queue is empty, i. What’s the probability distribution of express queue length? ii. What’s the expected express queue length? (Answer: 1.12 ) iii. What’s the variance of express queue length? (Answer: 0.6656 ) (e) Is express queue length independent of normal queue length?

4.5. A soccer club has two expensive star players recruited from overseas. Detailed match records give the bivariate distribution for (X1 , X2 ) below, where X1 is the number of points obtained in the game (2 for a win, 1 for a draw, 0 for a loss), and X2 is the number of stars playing in the game. The table below gives values of P(X1 = x1 , X2 = x2 ).

x2 0 1 2 0 0.15 0.12 0.06 x1 1 0.09 0.12 0.09 2 0.06 0.16 0.15

(a) Jono goes to a game hoping to see the club win the game and hoping to see at least one of the stars playing in the game. What is the chance that what Jono is hoping to see will actually happen? (Answer: 0.31 ) (b) i. Find the marginal distribution of X1 , the number of points obtained in a game. ii. Hence find the expected number of points obtained in a game. (Answer: 1.04 )

iii. Find the variance of the number of points obtained in a game. (Answer: 0.6984 ) (c) To Jono’s delight, when he arrives at the game he finds out that both stars will be playing in the game. What is the conditional distribution of points obtained in the game, given that both stars are playing? (d) Are X1 and X2 independent? Why? (e) What is the covariance between X1 and X2 ? (Answer: 0.18 )

4.6. A bivariate discrete random vector (X1 , X2 ) has joint probability mass function:

X2 0 1 2 −1 0.08 0.16 0.16 X1 1 0.04 0.06 0.10 2 0.08 0.18 0.14

(a) Calculate the expectation and variance of X1 . (Answer: 0.6; 1.84 ) (b) Determine the probability mass function of the random variable Y = X1 + X2 . (c) Find P(X1 = 1|X2 = 2) and P(X1 ≤ 1|X2 ≤ 1). (Answer: 1/4; 17/30 ) (d) Are X1 and X2 independent? Justify your answer.

4.7. On his way home from campus, John needs first to catch a bus, and then a train. Let X be the time (in minutes) that John spends waiting for a bus, and let Y be the time he spends waiting for a train. The vector (X, Y ) has joint probability density function: 1 −(x+y)/10 fX,Y (x, y) = e , x > 0, y > 0. (a) Determine the probability that John has to wait less than half an hour in total. (Answer: 1 − 4e−3 ) (b) Find the expected time that John spends waiting for a bus. (Answer: 10 minutes) (c) Find fY (y). (d) Find fY |X (y|x). (e) Are X and Y independent? Give reasons.

4.8. Let (X1 , X2 ) be a continuous random vector with joint density fX1 X2 (x1 , x2 ) = 6x1 1I{0<x1 <x2 <1} .

(a) Show that X2 has density fX2 (x2 ) = 3x22 , 0 < x2 < 1. (b) Prove that the conditional density of X1 given X2 = x2 is 2x1 fX1 |X2 (x1 |x2 ) = , for 0 < x1 < x2 x22 (c) Calculate P(X1 < 12 |X2 = x2 ) and E(X1 |X2 = x2 ). (Answer: 1/4x22 if 1/2 < x2 < 1, 1 if 0 < x2 < 1/2; 2x2 /3.) (d) Calculate P(X1 > 21 , X2 > 12 ). (Answer: 1/2)

4.9. Let (X1 , X2 ) be a continuous random vector with joint density function

fX1 X2 (x1 , x2 ) = 8x1 x2 1I{0<x1 <x2 <1} .

(a) Find fX1 (x) and fX2 (x2 ), the marginal density functions of X1 and X2 , respectively. (b) Calculate P(X1 < 21 , X2 < 12 ). (Answer: 1/16) (c) Are X1 and X2 independent?

4.10. Let (X, Y ) be a continuous random vector with joint density function

fX,Y (x, y) = 6(x − y)1I{0≤y≤x≤1} .

(a) Find the marginal densities of X and Y . (b) Calculate E(X|Y = y) and P(X < 21 |Y = y). (c) Find P(X > 2Y ) and P(X > 21 |X > 14 ). (Answer: 3/4 and 8/9 ) (d) Calculate Cov(X, Y ). (Answer: 1/80 )

4.11. Let (X1 , X2 ) be a random point distributed on the square   1 1 1 1 S = (x1 , x2 ) : − ≤ x1 ≤ , − ≤ x2 ≤ 2 2 2 2

with joint density ( 1, if (x1 , x2 ) ∈ S, fX1 ,X2 (x1 , x2 ) = 0, otherwise.

(a) Sketch the region S. (b) Compute the marginal densities fX1 (x1 ) and fX2 (x2 ). (c) Are X1 and X2 independent? Justify your answer. (d) Now suppose the square is rotated by 45◦ about the origin to form a diamond shape. Let (X1 , X2 ) have constant joint density 1 on the interior of this diamond and zero elsewhere. Sketch the new region. (e) Are the marginal densities still constant? Explain briefly. (f) Are X1 and X2 independent in this case? Why or why not?

4.12. Let X1 and X2 be independent random variables with means µ1 = E(X1 ), µ2 = E(X2 ), and variances σ12 = Var(X1 ), σ22 = Var(X2 ). Find Var(X1 X2 ) in terms of µ1 , µ2 , σ12 , σ22 .

4.13. For any random vector (X1 , X2 ), define the random variable E(X2 |X1 ) as a(X1 ), where a(x1 ) = E(X2 |X1 = x1 ). Prove that E{E(X2 |X1 )} = E(X2 ). This result is known as the Law of Iterated Expectations.

4.14. Let X and Y be independent random variables, each taking the values −1 or 1 with probability 1/2, and let Z = X × Y . Show that X, Y and Z are pairwise independent. Are they mutually independent?

4.15. A coin is tossed repeatedly and heads turns up on each toss with probability p. Let Hn and Tn be the numbers of heads and tails in n tosses. Show that, for any ϵ > 0,   P 2p − 1 − ϵ ≤ (Hn − Tn ) ≤ 2p − 1 + ϵ → 1 as n → ∞ n

(Hint: Use Chebyshev’s inequality and/or the Law of Large Numbers)

4.16. Show that for an arbitrary continuous r.v. X, Z 0 Z +∞ E(X) = − FX (x)dx + (1 − FX (x))dx −∞ 0

if at least one of the two terms is finite. (If both terms are infinite, the expectation is undefined, as for the Cauchy distribution).

4.17. Let X1 , X2 , . . . be a sequence of independent identically distributed continuous random variables. For n ≥ 2, define Xn as a record of the sequence if Xn > max(X1 , X2 , . . . , Xn−1 ), that is, if Xn is larger than each of the values observed so far.

a) Find the probability that X2 is a record. Use symmetry to obtain the answer without computation; (Answer: 1/2) b) Find the probability that Xn is a record, as a function of n. Again use symmetry. (Answer: 1/n)

c) Find a simple expression for the expected number of records that occur over the first m trials for any given integer m (X1 is not considered as a record). Show that this number in the limit m → +∞. is infinite P (Answer: m k=2 1/k → ∞ as m → ∞)

Let N1 be the index of the first record in the sequence.

d) Find P(N1 > n) for each n ≥ 2. (Answer: 1/n) e) Find E(N1 ). Contrast this result to part c). Comment and explain. (Answer: +∞)

4.18. Let X be the total biomass of a plant. Total biomass has mean µ and variance σ 2 . Consider the biomasses of a random sample of n plants, that is, consider n independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) variables X1 , X2 , . . . , Xn , all with mean µ and variance σ 2 . Find the mean and variance of: Pn (a) The total biomass of the n plants, Sn = i=1 Xi . The average biomass of the n plants, X̄n = n1 ni=1 Xi P (b) √  (c) Zn = n X̄n − µ .

4.19. Let X1 , X2 , . . . be a sequence of independent random variables with E(Xi ) = µ and Var(Xi ) = P σi2 (not necessarily all equal). Show that if n−2 ni=1 σi2 → 0, then X̄n → µ. This is a generP alisation of the Law of Large Numbers, which does not require the r.v. Xi ’s to be identically distributed (as they are allowed to have different variances).

4.20. Let X1 , X2 , . . . , Xn be independent and identically distributed continuous random variables with cumulative distribution function (cdf) FX . Define:

X(1) = min{X1 , . . . , Xn }, X(n) = max{X1 , . . . , Xn }.

Show that the joint cumulative distribution function of (X(1) , X(n) ) is ( FX (y)n , if x > y, FX(1) ,X(n) (x, y) = P(X(1) ≤ x, X(n) ≤ y) = n n . FX (y) − FX (y) − FX (x) , if x ≤ y.

5 Common distributions 5.1. We toss n coins, and each one shows heads with probability π ∈ (0, 1), independently of each of the others. Each coin which shows heads is tossed again. What is the probability mass function of the number of heads resulting from the second round of tosses? (Answer: -)

5.2. Calculate the moment generating function of the uniform distribution on [0, 1]. Obtain E(X) and Var(X) by differentiating. (Hint: Use L’Hospital’s rule.)

5.3. An uncooked spaghetti of length 1 is cut once at random. What is the probability that the longer piece is more than twice the length of the shorter piece? (Answer: 2/3)

5.4. A total of r keys are to be put, one Pat a time, in k boxes, with each key independently being put in box i with probability pi ( ki=1 pi = 1). Each time a key is put in a nonempty box, we say that a collision occurs. PkFind the expected number of collisions. r (Answer: E(X) = r − k + i=1 (1 − pi ) )

5.5. Airline passengers sometimes cancel their flights at the last minute. To maximise their profits by not wasting available seats, it is standard practice for airlines to overbook flights; i.e., to sell slightly more tickets than there are seats on the plane, on the assumption that not everyone will turn up for the flight. An airline sells 93 tickets on a flight that can only take 90 passengers. The airline believes that 5% of passengers won’t turn up for the flight.

(a) Propose a distribution that can be used to model the number of people who turn up for the flight. (b) What’s the expected number of people that turn up expecting a seat? (Answer: 88.35) (c) What’s the chance that at least one person with a ticket cannot get onto the plane? (Answer: 0.1504)

5.6. Consider the binomial distribution with n trials and probability π of success on each trial. What is the mode of this distribution? In other words, for which value of k is P (X = k) maximised? (Hint: Consider the ratios P(X = k + 1)/P(X = k), k = 0, . . . , n − 1.)

5.7. Two teams, A and B, play a series of games. If team A has probability 0.4 of winning each game, is it to its advantage to play the best three out of five games or the best four out of seven? Assume the outcomes of successive games are independent.

5.8. A basketball player has made 80% of his free throw shots during the season. Assuming he maintains this form in tonight’s game, find:

(a) the probability that he misses for the first time on his fifth attempt; (Answer: 0.082) (b) the probability that he makes his first basket on his fourth shot; (Answer: 0.0064)

(c) the probability that he makes his first basket on one of his first 3 shots; (Answer: 0.992) (d) the expected number of shots until he misses. (Answer: 5) 5.9. The number of yarn breaks per shift in a commercial fibre spinning machine is a Poisson random variable with λ = 3. Determine the probability of not experiencing any yarn break in a particular shift. What is the probability of experiencing more than 3 breaks per shift? (Answer: 0.0498 and 0.3528) 5.10. On average, 3.4 orders come into a take-away pizza shop every hour on a particular evening. Answer the following questions, making additional assumptions (within reason) as necessary to obtain an answer. (a) What is the chance that there is no order for take-away pizza in a given hour? (Answer: 0.033) (b) The pizza shop requires at least 3 orders per hour to break even. What is the chance that they will not break even for a given hour? (Answer: 0.340) (c) What is the expected waiting time between orders? (Answer: 17.65 minutes) (d) The pizza maker needs to duck out of the shop for 20 minutes. What is the chance that there will be no order while she is out? (Answer: 0.322) 5.11. The number of eggs laid by a particular bird per mating season is a Poisson random variable X, with parameter λ > 0. The probability that any such egg successfully develops into a hatchling is π ∈ (0, 1) (the probability that it does not survive is 1 − π). Call Y the random variable representing the number of surviving hatchlings, and determine its distribution. Assume mutual independence of the development of each egg. (Answer: Y ∼ P(λπ))  5.12. If X ∼ P(λ), find E X+1 . 5.13. Let T be an exponential random variable with parameter λ > 0. Let X be a discrete random variable defined as X = k if k ≤ T < k + 1, k = 0, 1, 2, . . .. Find the distribution of X. (Answer: X + 1 ∼ Geo(1 − e−λ )) 5.14. (a) If Z ∼ N (0, 1), determine P(Z < 1), P(Z > −2) and P(−2 < Z < 1). (Hint: Φ(1) = 0.8413; Φ(2) = 0.9772) (b) If X ∼ N (1, 9), calculate P(X < 2), P(X > 0) and P(0 < X < 2). (Hint: Φ(1/3) = 0.6306) 5.15. Most people think that the “normal" adult body temperature is 37◦ C. That figure, based on a 19th century study, has been challenged. In a 1992 article in the Journal of the American Medical Association, researchers reported that a more accurate figure may be 36.8◦ C. Further, the standard deviation appears to be around 0.4◦ C. Assume a normal distribution for body temperature for people in a given population.

(a) What fraction of people would be expected to have body temperatures above 37◦ C? (Hint: Φ(0.5) = 0.6915) (b) Below what temperature are the coolest 20% of people? (Hint: z0.2 = −0.8416) (Answer: 36.4634◦ C) (c) In what interval would you expect the body temperature of most people (say, 80% of people) to be? (Hint: z0.9 = 1.2816) (Answer: [36.2873, 37.3126])

5.16. The expectation is a measure of centrality of the distribution of a random variable, the variance a measure of its spread, and the skewness a measure of asymmetry. The kurtosis is another measure that is sometimes of interest: it tells us how heavy-tailed a distribution is. The kurtosis is defined as E [(X − µ)4 ] κ(X) = σ4 where E(X) = µ and Var(X) = σ 2 .

(a) Show that if Y ∼ N (0, σ 2 ) then κ(Y ) = 3. (Answer: - ) (Hint: Use moment generating functions) (b) Normal mixtures are commonly used to model distributions with ‘heavy-tails’; that is, which produce outliers (i.e., values ‘very far’ from µ) with substantial probability. Such distributions are useful for testing how robust different statistical procedures are to outliers. Suppose that X follows a particular type of normal mixture, with density: 1 x fX (x) = πϕ(x) + (1 − π) ϕ σ σ for −∞ < x < ∞ and 0 < π < 1, where ϕ(x) = √12π exp − 12 x2 is the density 

function of the standard normal distribution. i. Show that E[g(X)] = πE[g(Z)] + (1 − π)E[g(Y )] where Z ∼ N (0, 1) and Y = σZ ∼ N (0, σ 2 ), for any function g(x). ii. Hence find E(X), Var(X) and κ(X). iii. Show that the kurtosis of X is always at least as large as the kurtosis of Z ∼ N (0, 1).

5.17. Given a random sample of 10 observations drawn from a normal distribution with mean 100 and variance 25, compute the following probabilities regarding the sample mean X̄n :

(a) P(X̄n ≥ 100); (b) P(X̄n ≤ 100); (c) P(X̄n ≥ 104.5); (Answer: 0.0022 )

(d) P(96.9 ≤ X̄n ≤ 103.1); (Answer: 0.95) (e) P(98.4 ≤ X̄n ≤ 101.6). (Answer: 0.6884 )

(Hint: Φ(2.8461) = 0.9978, Φ(1.9606) = 0.9750, Φ(1.0119) = 0.8442)

5.18. A random sample (X1 , . . . , Xn ) of size n = 50 is taken from an unknown distribution FX with µ = 15 and σ 2 = 4.

(a) What is the standard deviation of the sample mean X̄n = n1 ni=1 Xi ? P (Answer: 0.2828) (b) If the sample size were reduced by 50%, what will be the new standard deviation of the sample mean X̄n/2 ? (Answer: 0.4) (c) To reduce the standard deviation to 50% of the value in (a), what sample size n will be needed? (Answer: 200)

5.19. As justification for its high price, an expensive brand of LED projector lamps has been purported to have an impressively long average lifetime of 5133 hours, with a standard deviation of 315 hours.

(a) In preparing to send a trial sample of n = 60 lamps to a customer, the manufacturing company wishes to accompany the shipment with a ‘factory specification’ stating the probability that the mean lifetime for this sample will be between the round numbers 5100 and 5150 hours. Determine this probability. (Hint: Φ(0.4180) = 0.6620, Φ(−0.8114) = 0.2085) (Answer: 0.4535) (b) In a follow-up to the shipment, information from end-use purchasers was used to track the actual lifetimes of the 60 lamps. The result showed an average lifetime less than 5000 hours. Compute the probability of obtaining such a low average lifetime if the lamps truly came from a collection (population) with µ = 5133 and σ = 315. (Hint: Φ(−3.2705) = 0.0005)

5.20. A drunkard executes a “random walk” in the following way: each minute he takes a step north or south, with probability 1/2 each, and his successive step directions are independent. His step length is 50 cm.

(a) Use the Central Limit Theorem to approximate the probability distribution of his location after 1 hour. (b) Where is he most likely to be after 1 hour?

(Note: This deceptively simple problem, effectively known as the drunkard’s walk, is among the most fundamental and widely studied stochastic processes.)

5.21. Suppose that a company ships packages that are variable in weight, with an average weight of 15 kg and a standard deviation of 10 kg. Assuming that the packages come from a large number of different customers so that it is reasonable to model their weights as independent random variables, find the probability that 100 packages will have a total weight exceeding 1700 kg. (Hint: Φ(2) = 0.9772)

5.22. The sum of two independent Poisson random variables is Poisson. Use this result, together with the Central Limit Theorem, to explain why for large λ, the Poisson distribution resembles a normal distribution. (You may want to plot the pmf of a Poisson distribution with large intensity λ to get convinced of this.)

5.23. Prove that the Central Limit Theorem extends to weighted averages in the following way: Suppose X1 , X2 , . . . are independent and identically distributed random variables with common mean µ = E(Xi ) < ∞ and common variance 0 < σ 2 = Var(Xi ) < ∞. Suppose that ai a1 , a2 , . . . an are a set of constants such that limn→∞ √P 2 = 0 for all i (and the notation P Pn i ai

Y −µ L pPn −→ N (0, 1) P σ i ai / i ai

as n → ∞. (See that, if all ai ’s are equal, we recover the usual CLT.) In your proof you may use thePfollowing result: consider a set of sequences, the nth sequence being {cn1 , cn2 , . . . , cnn }. If i cni = c and limn→∞ cni = 0 for all i then n Y lim (1 + cni x) = ecx . n→∞ i=1

6 Transformations of random variables 6.1. A continuous random variable X has density

fX (x) = 2x, 0 < x < 1.

Find fY (y) where Y = X 1/3 .

6.2. Given fX,Y (x, y) = 2(1 + x + y)−3 , x, y > 0 find the density function of X + Y .

6.3. (Related to 3.9) Consider a woodlot in which trees have basal diameter X (in metres) satisfying fX (x) = 1I{0<x<1} .

(a) Find the probability density function of basal area of tree trunks. (b) The trees have height 5 metres, and their timber currently fetches $30 per cubic metre when sold. Assuming cylindrical trunks, find the probability density function of the value of a tree in the woodlot. π (c) From these densities, verify the answers in 3.9 that the expected basal area is 12 (m2 ), 25π and the expected value of a tree is 2 ($).

6.4. Let X1 and X2 be independent Exponential random variables with parameter 1/4. We would like to find the distribution of Y1 = 12 (X1 − X2 ).

(a) Write down the joint density of X1 and X2 . (b) Find the joint density of Y1 and Y2 = X2 . (c) Hence find the distribution of Y1 .

(Note: Y1 is an example of what is known as the “double exponential" or Laplace distribution.)

6.5. Let X and Y be independent standard normal random variables. We will change to polar coordinates, in order to derive a means for generating random numbers from the bivariate normal distribution.

(a) Write down the joint distribution of X and Y . (b) Find the joint distribution of R and Θ, where: √   −1 Y R = X2 + Y 2 Θ = tan X

(c) Hence find the marginal distributions of R and Θ. (d) Are R and Θ independent? (e) Find the distribution of T = 1 − e−R /2 .

(f) Hence explain how you could use a standard uniform random number generator to generate a pair of independent random numbers from the standard normal distribution. 6.6. If X ∼ N (0, 1); i.e., fX (x) = √12π e−x /2 (x ∈ R), find the density of Y = |X| and hence find E|X|.

6.7. Let X and Y be independent U[0,1] -random variables. Determine the joint and marginal density functions of X/Y and X − Y .

6.8. A point is chosen at random in the unit square:

{(x, y) : 0 ≤ x ≤ 1, 0 ≤ y ≤ 1}.

(a) Let R denote the distance from the point to the nearest edge of the unit square. What is the density function of R? (b) Find E(R). (c) Let S denote the distance from the point to the nearest vertex (i.e. corner) of the unit square. What is the density function of S? (d) Find E(S).

6.9. Let (X1 , . . . , Xn ) be a random sample drawn from the Poisson distribution with mean 1, and let X̄n denote the sample mean. Approximate the distribution of √ p  Yn = n X̄n − 1

for large n.

(a) Use the delta method to find the asymptotic distribution of X̄1n . (b) Find the asymptotic distribution of log(X̄n ).

(a) Let Pbn = X̄n be the sample proportion (Section 5.5). Identify its asymptotic distribution. (b) Use the delta method  to find the asymptotic distribution of the so-called log-odds transformation log 1−P̂nP̂ . n p (c) Find the asymptotic distribution of the arcsine transformation arcsin( P̂n ).

6.12. Let X1 , . . . , Xn be a random sample from an exponential distribution with rate parameter λ > 0.

(a) Find the asymptotic distribution of X̄n . . 1 (b) Let Λ bn = . Use the delta method to find the asymptotic distribution of Λ X̄n b n.

7 Introduction to statistical inference 7.1. Let (X1 , . . . , Xn ) ∼ P(λ).

(a) Let n 1X X̄n = Xi n i=1

be an estimator of λ. Find the bias, standard error and Mean Squared Error of X̄n . (b) Let X1 + 2X2 + . . . + nXn Tbn = . 1 + 2 + ... + n Compute the bias and MSE of Tbn as an estimator of λ. (c) Compare Tbn to X̄n in terms of MSE. Which estimator is preferable? Intuitively, why is one better than the other?

7.2. Let X1 , . . . , Xn be a random sample from the U[0,θ] -distribution, with θ > 0 unknown.

(a) Let Tbn = 2X̄n be an estimator of θ. Find bθ (Tbn ) and MSEθ (Tbn ). (b) Let Ten = max(X1 , . . . , Xn ) be another estimator of θ. Find bθ (Ten ) and MSEθ (Ten ). (c) For which sample sizes is Ten better than Tbn ? (d) Show that both Tbn = 2X̄n and Ten = max(X1 , . . . , Xn ) are consistent estimators of θ.

7.3. Let (X1 , . . . , Xn ) be a random sample of Bernoulli random variables with parameter π.

(a) Let n Y X Tbn = X̄n = where Y = Xi n i=1

be an estimator of π. Find bπ (Tbn ) and MSEπ (Tbn ). (b) Let p Y + n/4 Tn = e √ . n+ n be another estimator of π. Find bπ (Ten ) and MSEπ (Ten ). (c) Graph MSEπ (Tbn ) and MSEπ (Ten ) against π on the same set of axes in the case n = 4 (possibly with the assistance of a computer). For which values of π is Ten better than Tbn ? (d) Repeat (c) for n = 400.

7.4. (Related to question 6.12.) Let X1 , . . . , Xn be i.i.d. from an Exponential distribution with parameter λ > 0. The expectation of the Exp(λ)-distribution is E(Xi ) = 1/λ, so here the sample mean X̄n converges to 1/λ as n → ∞. Thus, a natural estimator for λ may be Tbn = 1/X̄n .

(a) Is Tbn = 1/X̄n unbiased for λ? If not, is the bias positive or negative?

(b) Is Tbn a consistent estimator of λ? Justify. 7.5. Let (X1 , . . . , Xn ) ∼ FX . For y ∈ R fixed, define n 1X FX;n (y) = b 1I{Xi ≤y} , n i=1

the empirical cdf at y.

(a) Show that E[FbX;n (y)] = FX (y). (b) Compute the variance of FbX;n (y). (c) Deduce the Mean Square Error of FbX;n (y), and therefore the consistency FbX;n (y) as an estimator of FX (y). 7.6. Let (X1 , . . . , Xn ) ∼ N (µ, σ 2 ) with known σ 2 . Compute the Fisher Information for µ. Is the sample mean X̄n an efficient estimator of µ? Justify.

8 Estimation methods 8.1. Let (X1 , . . . , Xn ) ∼ P(λ). Find:

(a) the moment estimator for λ, (b) the maximum likelihood estimator for λ, (c) the Fisher information I(λ). 8.2. Determine the maximum likelihood estimator of θ when (X1 , . . . , Xn ) ∼ fθ , where:

(a) fθ (x) = θe−θx , x > 0; θ > 0. (b) fθ (x) = θxθ−1 , 0 < x < 1; θ > 0, (c) fθ (x) = eθ−x , x ≥ θ, (d) fθ (x) = 1, θ < x < θ + 1.

8.3. (a) Let (X1 , . . . , Xn ) be independent and identically distributed random variables with density   1 |x − θ| fθ (x) = exp − , x ∈ R, 2b b where b > 0 is known and θ ∈ R is an unknown parameter. This is called the Laplace distribution. Find the maximum likelihood estimator (MLE) of θ. (b) Now consider the case where both θ ∈ R and b > 0 are unknown parameters. Find the maximum likelihood estimator of (θ, b).

8.4. I
